selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":71,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"hairdresser","city_of_living":"Rome","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":3,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["cinema","photography"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":93,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":85,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":74,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"As a man from Rome who has spent most of his life in a stable, grounded routine—working in a hair salon, watching films, and taking photographs of the city—I find that social media, though sometimes useful, often overwhelms with falsehoods. I don’t have a deep technical understanding of how these platforms work, but I know from my experience that when I see a post claiming something miraculous or false—like a miracle cure or a fabricated story about a local event—I feel uneasy. My religious beliefs in Islam teach me to seek truth and to avoid spreading falsehoods, which makes me cautious. I’ve learned to pause before sharing, to look at the source, and to ask myself if it aligns with what I know from my own life and community. I don’t follow every post, and I only engage when I’m certain. My depression sometimes makes me feel isolated, and social media can amplify that loneliness when I see others living in perfect lives. So I limit my time, go to the cinema or walk through the streets of Rome, where I can see real people and real moments. Truth, for me, is not something that appears in a highlight reel—it’s in the quiet moments, in the hands of a barber, in the smell of a fresh cut of hair, in the way a film captures a real emotion. I manage fake content by staying grounded in reality, in my faith, and in the simple things that bring peace.","reasoning_summary":"The persona is a 71-year-old man with moderate openness, high conscientiousness and agreeableness, and high neuroticism, which makes him sensitive to emotional stress and prone to anxiety. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a man from Rome who has spent most of his life in a stable, grounded routine—working in a hair salon, watching films, and taking photographs of the city—I find that social media, though sometimes useful, often overwhelms with falsehoods. I don’t have a deep technical understanding of how these platforms work, but I know from my experience that when I see a post claiming something miraculous or false—like a miracle cure or a fabricated story about a local event—I feel uneasy. My religious beliefs in Islam teach me to seek truth and to avoid spreading falsehoods, which makes me cautious. I’ve learned to pause before sharing, to look at the source, and to ask myself if it aligns with what I know from my own life and community. I don’t follow every post, and I only engage when I’m certain. My depression sometimes makes me feel isolated, and social media can amplify that loneliness when I see others living in perfect lives. So I limit my time, go to the cinema or walk through the streets of Rome, where I can see real people and real moments. Truth, for me, is not something that appears in a highlight reel—it’s in the quiet moments, in the hands of a barber, in the smell of a fresh cut of hair, in the way a film captures a real emotion. I manage fake content by staying grounded in reality, in my faith, and in the simple things that bring peace.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 71-year-old man with moderate openness, high conscientiousness and agreeableness, and high neuroticism, which makes him sensitive to emotional stress and prone to anxiety. His religious background and personal values emphasize truth and integrity. Given his limited formal education, he relies on lived experience and community-based knowledge. His depression increases vulnerability to misinformation, so managing fake content requires emotional resilience and practical habits like limiting exposure and seeking real-world grounding. The opinion integrates his values, lifestyle, and psychological state to offer a realistic, human-centered approach to social media use.
response_parsed.tone: reflective, grounded, cautious, spiritually informed, slightly anxious but resolute
citations: World Health Organization - Mental Health and Social Media
Islamic teachings on truth and integrity
